title: Radioactive Contamination Control for NR Work
narrationScript: Most of this course treats radiation as an external hazard — a field you step into and out of. Neutron radiographic work adds a second problem: radioactive material itself, loose on surfaces, on clothing, or in the air. That is contamination, and unlike a field it travels with you and keeps delivering dose until it is removed or decays. This lesson shows where contamination comes from in NR work — activated parts and fixtures, transfer screens that are radioactive by design, and the rare leaking californium source. Then we dress for it: full anti-contamination clothing, taped closures, double gloves and shoe covers — remembering that fabric stops contamination, never penetrating radiation. We control it with posted boundaries, step-off pads, strict hygiene rules, surveys of everything that leaves, and a frisk every time you exit. And when control fails, we clean up: gentle soap-and-water decontamination for skin, damp outside-in methods for surfaces, all of it under the RSO's direction, and nothing released until a survey proves it clean.

sections.heading: Contamination: The Hazard That Travels With You
sections.body:
Everything in this course so far has treated radiation as an **external** hazard — a field that exists around a source, falls off with distance, and ends when the source is shielded or you walk away. **Radioactive contamination** is different: it is radioactive material itself — particles, flakes, dust, or liquid — sitting **where it is not wanted**. A radiation field stays at the job site. Contamination rides out on your boots, your sleeves, and your tools, and if you breathe or swallow it, it rides **inside your body**, delivering dose continuously until it decays or is removed.

Keep the two words straight:

- **Irradiated** means radiation passed through you. At radiography energies, gamma, X, and beta radiation do not make you or the part radioactive — leave the field and the dose stops.
- **Contaminated** means radioactive material is **on or in** you. The dose continues — and you spread the material to everything you touch.

Contamination comes in three working forms. **Fixed** contamination is bound to a surface; it cannot rub off, and you find it with a direct instrument survey — a frisk. **Removable (loose)** contamination transfers to whatever touches it; it is measured by wiping a smear pad across about **100 square centimeters** of surface and counting the pad on a sensitive instrument. **Airborne** contamination is suspended in the air, detected by air sampling, and is the most dangerous form because it is inhalable. The intake pathways are inhalation, ingestion, absorption through intact skin, and entry through wounds — and any intake adds **internal dose** to your record on top of everything your badge reads externally.

sections.heading: Why NR Work Has a Contamination Problem
sections.body:
Field gamma radiography is engineered to be a contamination-free trade: the iridium-192 or cobalt-60 lives inside a welded special-form capsule that is **leak tested at intervals not exceeding six months** (10 CFR 34.27), precisely so the hazard stays external. Neutron radiographic work — performed at reactor, accelerator, and californium-252 facilities — cannot make that promise, for three reasons:

- **Activation is constant.** Everything living in or near the thermal-neutron beam — collimators, filters, fixtures, and the test parts themselves — undergoes neutron capture and becomes radioactive (the cobalt-59 to cobalt-60 story from Lesson 1.1). Activated hardware is an external dose source, and activated surface films and corrosion products can flake off as removable contamination. Reactor-based facilities add activated corrosion products circulating in pool and cooling water.
- **Transfer screens are radioactive on purpose.** In the transfer technique, the beam image is captured in an indium or dysprosium foil; the activated foil — indium-116m (half-life about 54 minutes) or dysprosium-165 (about 2.33 hours) — is then carried to a cassette, where its own decay radiation exposes the film. The radiographer is, by design, handling a freshly activated radioactive source: tongs, planned moves, and decay time are part of the technique.
- **Sources can leak.** Californium-252 decays by alpha emission and spontaneous fission, so a breached Cf-252 capsule scatters alpha emitters and fission products — the severe internal hazard from Lesson 1.1, where alpha inside the body carries a quality factor of about 20.

The result: NR work runs under health-physics coverage, with defined contamination zones, routine smear surveys, and exit monitoring — disciplines this lesson now makes yours.

sections.caption: Where contamination originates in neutron radiographic work — activation, deliberately activated transfer screens, and source leakage — and the three forms it takes: fixed, removable, and airborne.

sections.heading: Clothing Requirements: Dressing for Contamination Work
sections.body:
Where contamination is possible, skin and street clothes are never the working surface. The standard dress-out in **anti-contamination clothing (anti-Cs)**:

- **Full-body coveralls** — cotton or a spun-bonded fabric such as Tyvek — covering all personal clothing.
- **Head cover or hood**, because hair collects loose material and hands later carry it to the face.
- **Double gloves**: thin inner gloves under outer work gloves, so the dirty outer pair comes off without ever exposing bare skin.
- **Double shoe covers** over work boots — the floor is usually the most contaminated surface in the area.
- **Tape at wrists and ankles**, sealing the glove-to-sleeve and shoe-cover-to-cuff joints.

A torn or soaked garment is exchanged immediately — wet fabric wicks contamination straight through to skin. Cuts and scrapes are covered with a waterproof dressing before entry; broken skin is an open door for intake. Personal items — phone, gum, cigarettes, jewelry — stay outside.

**Respirators are not casual equipment.** Airborne hazards are controlled first by containment and ventilation (10 CFR 20.1701); when respiratory protection is genuinely needed, it is NIOSH-certified equipment issued under the licensee's written respiratory-protection program — fit testing, medical screening, training — per 10 CFR 20.1702 and 20.1703. A hardware-store dust mask is not respiratory protection.

Getting anti-Cs **off** is the half of the job that decides whether they worked: dirtiest items first, clean hands touching only inside surfaces, coveralls rolled off inside-out, shoe covers removed one foot at a time as you step across to the clean side, **inner gloves last** — then straight to the frisker. Your facility's written procedure prescribes the exact sequence; follow it every time.

sections.title: Fabric stops contamination — not radiation
sections.body: A complete, perfectly taped set of anti-Cs does nothing about the gamma and neutron field around an open beam port or a freshly activated part — cotton and Tyvek attenuate penetrating radiation by an amount too small to matter. Protective clothing controls the **contamination pathway**; **time, distance, and shielding** control external dose, exactly as before. The most dangerous habit in contamination work is feeling protected because you are fully dressed.

sections.caption: Representative anti-contamination ensemble — what each item does and how it comes off.
rows: Keep loose material off skin and street clothing
Roll off inside-out, touching only the inner surface
Hands are the highest-contact point; the outer pair takes the contamination
Outer pair off early; inner gloves are the last item off
Floor contact — usually the most contaminated surface
Off one foot at a time, stepping across to the clean side
Keeps material out of hair, where hands later carry it to the face
Off without shaking — resuspension is the enemy
Seals the glove-to-sleeve and shoe-cover-to-cuff gaps
Peel slowly before glove and coverall removal — no snapping
Inhalation protection under the licensee's written program — engineering controls come first
Stays on until clothing is off, protecting the airway while doffing stirs material

sections.heading: Contamination Control in Practice
sections.body:
Contamination control is boundary discipline plus a handful of unbreakable habits:

- **The zone is defined and posted.** Boundaries are marked with rope, tape, or rails and conspicuously posted — 10 CFR 20.1902 prescribes the warning postings for radioactive-material and airborne-radioactivity areas — with a single control point for entry and exit.
- **The step-off pad is the airlock.** Doffing happens at the pad, and clean shoes land only on the clean side.
- **Nothing crosses the line unproven.** Tools, fixtures, samples, and parts leaving the area are surveyed — direct frisk plus smears — and released only within the limits in your license and procedures.
- **Mouth and face are off-limits.** No eating, drinking, smoking, chewing, or applying cosmetics anywhere contamination is possible — each one turns loose surface activity into an ingestion intake. Keep hands away from your face and wash before breaks.
- **Handle activated items like sources.** Tongs and remote handling for anything fresh out of the beam, and let short-lived activity decay before hands-on work — with indium's 54-minute half-life, an hour of patience removes roughly half the problem.
- **Contain at the source.** Catch trays, plastic sleeving, and ventilation toward filters beat cleanup every time. Damp-wipe surfaces; **never dry-sweep** — a broom turns removable contamination into airborne contamination.
- **Frisk out, every time.** Probe about a half inch from the body, moving slowly — an inch or two per second — hands and feet at minimum, whole body where the procedure requires. If the frisker alarms: stop, stay, and call the RSO. Walking away with contamination is how it reaches your car, your home, and your family.

Routine smear surveys on a documented schedule close the loop, proving day after day that control is holding.

sections.caption: A contamination area built for control: posted boundary, tongs-only handling of activated parts, radwaste collection at the point of use, a step-off pad straddling the line, and the personnel frisker as the final gate.

sections.heading: Contamination Cleanup: People, Surfaces, and Spills
sections.body:
When a frisk alarms or a spill happens, the first minutes follow one pattern: **stop** the work and the spread — right the container, set the item down, close the door; **warn** the people around you and keep traffic out; **isolate** — rope off generously, because a boundary is cheap to shrink and expensive to chase; **minimize** further exposure and disturbance — and if airborne contamination is suspected, leave the room so ventilation can be managed deliberately. Then **notify the RSO**: cleanup proceeds under RSO direction, not improvisation.

**Personnel decontamination is gentle by design.** Wash the contaminated spot with mild soap and **lukewarm** water for two to three minutes, resurvey, and repeat as the RSO directs. Never use stiff brushes, hot water, or solvents — abraded, heat-flushed, or solvent-softened skin absorbs radioactive material instead of releasing it. Flush eyes and wounds promptly with running water, and remember that a serious injury is treated first: medical care always outranks decontamination. If material may have been inhaled or swallowed, the RSO arranges **bioassay** — whole-body counting or sample analysis — to measure the intake and assign the internal dose.

**Surface cleanup works damp, from the outside edges inward**, with single-pass wipes folded to a fresh face each stroke; HEPA-filtered vacuums handle dry residue, and liquids are kept to a minimum because every drop becomes radioactive waste. Used wipes, gloves, and anti-Cs are bagged, labeled, and disposed of as radioactive waste under the license (10 CFR 20, Subpart K).

**Done means surveyed — not 'looks clean.'** The area returns to service only when direct and smear surveys meet the limits in your license and procedures — many licenses derive surface values from legacy NRC guidance, such as 1,000 dpm per 100 square centimeters of removable beta-gamma activity — and the event is documented: location, levels found, levels after cleanup, and where the waste went.

figures.title: Contamination sources and forms in NR work
Contamination area layout and exit lane

interactive.title: Last Stop Before You Leave
interactive.intro: Your anti-Cs came off in the correct order at the step-off pad and your inner gloves are in the waste bag. Click the one station you must still use before walking away from this contamination area.

regions.label: Posting sign
regions.feedback: Postings do their work on the way in — they tell you what the area is and what is required to enter it. A sign cannot tell you whether you are clean on the way out.

regions.label: Tongs and activated parts
regions.feedback: Tongs and remote handling keep dose and contamination off your hands during the job — but they prove nothing about your skin and clothing now that the work is done.

regions.label: Radwaste container
regions.feedback: Bagging your used anti-Cs there was right — but a closed waste container only controls the material you already removed. It says nothing about what may still be on you.

regions.label: Step-off pad
regions.feedback: You are already standing on it. The pad marks the clean/dirty line and gives doffing a controlled place to happen — but it cannot detect contamination.

regions.label: Personnel frisker
regions.feedback: Correct. The exit frisk is the only step that proves you are clean: probe about a half inch from the body, moving an inch or two per second — hands and feet at minimum, whole body where the procedure requires it. If it alarms, stay put and call the RSO; never carry the problem out of the area.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement captures the difference between being irradiated and being contaminated?
options: Irradiation delivers dose only while you are in the field; contamination is radioactive material on or in the body that keeps delivering dose until it is removed or decays
Irradiation by gamma rays makes the body radioactive, while contamination does not
Contamination involves only alpha emitters, while irradiation involves only gamma rays
The two terms are interchangeable in radiation protection
checkQuestions.explanation: Gamma, X, and beta radiation at radiography energies pass through without making you radioactive — leave the field and the dose stops. Contamination travels with you, doses you continuously, and spreads to whatever you touch.

checkQuestions.question: A technician in a full set of taped anti-contamination clothing must work near an open thermal-neutron beam port. What protection do the anti-Cs provide against the beam?
options: They attenuate the neutron flux by about half
Essentially none — protective clothing controls contamination only, so time, distance, and shielding still govern external dose
They stop the neutrons but not the gamma component
Full protection, as long as every closure is properly taped
checkQuestions.explanation: Cotton and Tyvek attenuate penetrating gamma and neutron radiation by an amount too small to matter. Anti-Cs keep radioactive material off skin and clothing — external dose is still managed with time, distance, and shielding.

checkQuestions.question: Which doffing practice at the step-off pad is correct?
options: Remove the inner gloves first so bare hands are free to untie everything else
Pull the coveralls off so the contaminated outer surface rolls against your skin — it is faster
Roll the coveralls off inside-out touching only inner surfaces, remove shoe covers one foot at a time stepping to the clean side, take the inner gloves off last, then frisk
Skip the exit frisk whenever your pocket dosimeter shows no added dose for the shift
checkQuestions.explanation: Doffing keeps clean hands on clean (inside) surfaces, takes the dirtiest layers off first, and saves the inner gloves for last. A pocket dosimeter integrates external exposure — it cannot detect contamination, so the frisk is never optional.

checkQuestions.question: An exit frisk finds contamination on the back of your hand. The correct first response is to:
options: Scrub the spot with a stiff brush and the hottest water you can stand
Wipe the spot with a solvent, which dissolves contamination fastest
Put your glove back on and finish the shift before dealing with it
Notify the RSO and wash gently with mild soap and lukewarm water for a few minutes, then resurvey — repeating as directed
checkQuestions.explanation: Abrasion, hot water, and solvents all drive radioactive material into or through the skin instead of off it. Skin decontamination is gentle soap-and-lukewarm-water cycles with a resurvey after each, under RSO direction.

checkQuestions.question: In transfer-technique neutron radiography, indium or dysprosium screens are handled with tongs after they leave the beam because:
options: the beam deliberately activates them — indium-116m (half-life about 54 minutes) and dysprosium-165 (about 2.33 hours) are genuinely radioactive when carried to the film
the foils are chemically toxic on skin contact
residual neutrons continue streaming off the foil for several days
the foils are fragile, and oils from bare hands would ruin the image
checkQuestions.explanation: Transfer screens work precisely because they become radioactive in the beam and then expose the film with their own decay radiation. The radiographer is carrying a real radioactive source and treats it like one: tongs, distance, and timing planned around the short half-life.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024), Appendix A §5.0–5.1 (NR work practices: radioactive contamination, clothing requirements, contamination control, contamination cleanup)
10 CFR Part 20 — Standards for Protection Against Radiation (surveys and monitoring, posting, respiratory protection §§20.1701–20.1703, waste disposal Subpart K)
10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography (sealed-source integrity and leak testing, §34.27)
ASTM E748 — Standard Guide for Thermal Neutron Radiography of Materials
